title: W5 A Linear Functions
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 5A    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/17
 Vector Spaces




                2/17
                  Euclidean Spaces as Vector Spaces

Recall that Rn is a vector space. For all x, y, z ∈ Rn and scalars α, β ∈ R:
  ◦ x + y ∈ Rn ,

  ◦ y + x = x + y,

  ◦ (x + y) + z = x + (y + z),

  ◦ there exists 0 such that 0 + x = x,

  ◦ for every x ∈ Rn there exists an element (−x) ∈ Rn such that
    x + (−x) = 0,

  ◦ α x ∈ Rn ,

  ◦ α (x + y) = α x + α y,

  ◦ (α + β) x = α x + β x.




                                                                               3/17
                           Linear Combinations



A linear combination of the family of vectors {x1 , x2 , . . . , xK } in Rn is
the vector
                       α1 x1 + α2 x2 + . . . + αK xK .


 Definition 1

    A collection of vectors {x1 , x2 , . . . , xK } is linearly independent if
    the system of equations

                     α1 x1 + α2 x2 + . . . + αK xK = 0

    implies α1 = α2 = . . . = αK = 0.




                                                                                 4/17
                        Linear Independence


Example 1

  (a) The collection {(1, 0), (0, 1)} in R2 is linearly independent.

 (b) The collection {(1, 0), (2, 2)} in R2 is also linearly independent.




                                                                           5/17
                          Linear Independence


Example 1

  (a) The collection {(1, 0), (0, 1)} in R2 is linearly independent.

 (b) The collection {(1, 0), (2, 2)} in R2 is also linearly independent.



     2                                            2


     1                                            1



              1      2                                     1      2




                                                                           5/17
                         Linear Independence

Example 2

  (a) The collection {(1, 0), (−1, 0)} in R2 is not linearly independent.

 (b) Neither is the collection {(1, 0), (0, 1), (2, 2)}.
        ◦ We can linearly combined (1, 0) and (0, 1) to obtain (2, 2).




                            2


                            1



                                      1      2



                                                                            6/17
                          Linear Independence

Example 2

  (a) The collection {(1, 0), (−1, 0)} in R2 is not linearly independent.

 (b) Neither is the collection {(1, 0), (0, 1), (2, 2)}.
         ◦ We can linearly combined (1, 0) and (0, 1) to obtain (2, 2).

  (c) The collection {(1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)} in R3 is linearly
      independent.


                             2


                             1



                                       1       2



                                                                            6/17
                                 Standard Basis



Notation: let ei ∈ Rn denote the vector whose all components are zero
except for its i’s component, which is one.

                           ei = (0, . . . , 0, 1, 0, . . . , 0)

The collection of vectors {e1 , . . . , en } is know as the standard basis for Rn .
  ◦ Any vector x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) in Rn can be expressed as a linear
     combination of {e1 , . . . , en }:

                     x = (x1 , . . . , xn )
                         = x1 e1 + . . . + xi ei + . . . + xn en .

  ◦ We sometimes refer to ei as a directional vector.




                                                                                      7/17
 Linear Functions




                   8/17
                          Linear Functions


Example 3

 Consider the following functions.
  (a) f : R → R defined by f (x) = a x, for a ∈ R.

 (b) g : Rn → R defined by g(x) = a · x, for a ∈ Rn .

  (c) h : Rn → Rm defined by h(x) = A x, for the m × n matrix A.




                                                                   9/17
                            Linear Functions


 Example 3

   Consider the following functions.
    (a) f : R → R defined by f (x) = a x, for a ∈ R.

    (b) g : Rn → R defined by g(x) = a · x, for a ∈ Rn .

    (c) h : Rn → Rm defined by h(x) = A x, for the m × n matrix A.



These are examples of linear functions (or linear transformations).
  ◦ Note that f (x + y) = a(x + y) = ax + ay = f (x) + f (y).

  ◦ Note also that f (α x) = a(α x) = α(a x) = αf (x).

  ◦ The same holds for g and h.




                                                                      9/17
                              Linear Functions


In what follows, let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.


 Definition 2

   A function T : Rn → Rm is linear if for all x, y ∈ Rn and α ∈ R,
    (a) T (x + y) = T (x) + T (y),
    (b) T (α x) = α T (x).




                                                                      10/17
                              Linear Functions


In what follows, let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.


 Definition 2

   A function T : Rn → Rm is linear if for all x, y ∈ Rn and α ∈ R,
    (a) T (x + y) = T (x) + T (y),
    (b) T (α x) = α T (x).



 Exercise 1

   Show that all the functions in Example 3 are linear.




                                                                      10/17
                Characterisation of Linear Functions




The previous exercise can be strengthened to obtain the following:


 Proposition 1

   A function T : Rn → Rm is linear if and only if there exists an m × n
   matrix A such that
                     T (x) = A x,      for all x ∈ Rn .


Proof Omitted.




                                                                           11/17
              Characterisation of Linear Functions


Remarks
 ◦ Here it’s important to pay attention to the dimension of A.
     ◦ T : Rn → Rm requires an m × n matrix A.




                                                                 12/17
               Characterisation of Linear Functions


Remarks
  ◦ Here it’s important to pay attention to the dimension of A.
      ◦ T : Rn → Rm requires an m × n matrix A.

  ◦ If n = 1 and m = 1, then A is a 1 × 1 matrix: A = a and thus

                                 T (x) = a x.

  ◦ If n ≥ 2 and m = 1, then A is a 1 × n matrix: A = a⊤ and thus

                       T (x) = A x = a⊤ x = a · x.

Which notation one chooses is a matter of convenience. But be careful!




                                                                         12/17
                            Affine Functions


In what follows, let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.


 Definition 3

   A function T : Rn → Rm is affine if it is of the form

                            T (x) = A x + b,

   for an m × n matrix A and vector b ∈ Rm .




                                                           13/17
                              Affine Functions


In what follows, let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.


 Definition 3

    A function T : Rn → Rm is affine if it is of the form

                              T (x) = A x + b,

    for an m × n matrix A and vector b ∈ Rm .


An affine function which fixes the origin (i.e. b = 0) is just a linear function.
We can think of an affine function as a linear function that is “shifted”.




                                                                                    13/17
                  Affine Functions are Continuous

Recall that we let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.

 Proposition 2

   If T : Rn → Rm is an affine function, then T is continuous.




                                                                 14/17
                  Affine Functions are Continuous

Recall that we let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.

 Proposition 2

   If T : Rn → Rm is an affine function, then T is continuous.


Proof   Assume that m = 1 (the other case is similar).
  ◦ Consider T (x) = a · x + b, for some a, b ∈ Rn .

  ◦ Fix arbitrary y ∈ Rn and let {y k }∞
                                       k=1 be a sequence converging to y.

  ◦ We show that T (y k ) → T (y).




                                                                            14/17
                  Affine Functions are Continuous

Recall that we let n ≥ 1 and m ≥ 1.

 Proposition 2

   If T : Rn → Rm is an affine function, then T is continuous.


Proof   Assume that m = 1 (the other case is similar).
  ◦ Consider T (x) = a · x + b, for some a, b ∈ Rn .

  ◦ Fix arbitrary y ∈ Rn and let {y k }∞
                                       k=1 be a sequence converging to y.

  ◦ We show that T (y k ) → T (y).

  ◦ For any y k , we have

     |T (y k ) − T (y)| = |a · y k − a · y|
                       = |a · (y k − y)| ≤ ∥a∥ ∥y k − y∥.




                                                                            14/17
                       Application: 2-Player Games


Anne and Bill are playing a round of Rock-Paper-Scissors.
  ◦ Rules of the game are standard.
  ◦ Anne gets 1 dollar if she wins, 0 dollars in a tie, and pays 1 dollar if she loses.
  ◦ Payoffs to Bill are symmetric negative.


                                                   Bill
                                       rock      paper      scissors
                            rock        0, 0      −1, 1      1, −1
                Anne       paper       1, −1       0, 0      −1, 1
                          scissors     −1, 1      1, −1       0, 0


Claim There is no pure strategy NE for this game.




                                                                                          15/17
                               Mixed Strategies



Anne and Bill are playing a simultaneous finite game.
  ◦ Anne has m pure strategies, Bill has n pure strategies.
  ◦ Let aij denote Anne’s payoff if she chooses strategy i = 1, . . . , m while
    Bill chooses strategy j = 1, . . . , n.

A mixed strategy for Anne is x = (x1 , . . . , xm ) such that xi ≥ 0 for all
                    P
i = 1, . . . , m and i xi = 1.

Anne’s set of mixed strategies is the m-dimensional simplex
                     n          Xm                    o
              ∆m = x ∈ R m :           xi = 1, xi ≥ 0 .
                                              i=1




                                                                                  16/17
                                 Expected Payoffs


Given mixed strategies x = (x1 , . . . , xm ) for Anne and y = (y1 , . . . , yn ) for
Bill, Anne’s expected payoff is
                    XX                                               
      E uA (x, y) =        aij Prob Anne chooses i and Bill chooses j
                        i   j
                       XX
                   =            aij xi yj
                        i   j




                                                                                        17/17
                                 Expected Payoffs


Given mixed strategies x = (x1 , . . . , xm ) for Anne and y = (y1 , . . . , yn ) for
Bill, Anne’s expected payoff is
                    XX                                               
      E uA (x, y) =        aij Prob Anne chooses i and Bill chooses j
                        i   j
                       XX                     X
                   =            aij xi yj =         xi aij yj = x⊤A y.
                        i   j                 i,j



 Proposition 3

    For any mixed strategy y chosen by Bill, there is a mixed strategy
    x∗ (y) that maximizes Anne’s expected payoffs.


Proof    Omitted.
